original_hypothesis.title: addManagedNFT Allows Duplicate NFT Entries Creating Double-Withdrawal and Release Issues
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: addManagedNFT only checks that the contract owns the NFT but does not check whether the NFT is already in the ManagedNFTs array. If the owner calls addManagedNFT(nft) twice, the same NFT address appears twice in ManagedNFTs. During withdrawFromManagedNFTs, the first withdrawal drains the NFT's balance; the second withdrawal for the same NFT gets 0 (wasting gas). When releaseManagedNFT is called, only one occurrence is removed (the loop breaks), leaving a stale duplicate entry. Subsequent withdrawal cycles hit the stale entry (NFT no longer owned), causing reverts.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Combined with the require(true) bug in releaseManagedNFT, this creates a compounding state corruption. The stale entry from an incomplete duplicate removal cannot be detected or removed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ManagedNFTs.push(nftContract) without checking for existing entry; no deduplication

preconditions: Owner calls addManagedNFT with same NFT address more than once
Contract owns the NFT at time of each call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No duplicate check before push to ManagedNFTs
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: releaseManagedNFT with break only removes first occurrence, leaving stale duplicate
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Mapping to track managed NFT membership, or duplicate check before push

analysis_questions: Can addManagedNFT be called for the same NFT twice?
What is the impact of a stale ManagedNFTs entry on withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Call addManagedNFT(nft) twice, verify ManagedNFTs contains duplicate
Release the NFT, verify one entry remains
Attempt withdrawal cycle, verify revert on stale entry

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner accidentally adds same NFT twice. When releasing it, releaseManagedNFT breaks after finding and removing the first occurrence. A stale duplicate entry remains. Next withdrawal cycle tries to withdraw from the released (unowned) NFT and reverts, blocking revenue collection.
prerequisites: Owner adds same NFT address to ManagedNFTs more than once

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Withdrawal DoS blocking revenue collection from all managed NFTs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders affected by blocked distributions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult — no way to remove stale entry without owning the NFT again

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for duplicate addManagedNFT calls

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via ManagedNFTs array inspection for duplicates

validation_result.impact_summary: Adding the same NFT twice leaves a stale entry after release, permanently bricking all future revenue withdrawals.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `addManagedNFT` function verifies that the contract owns the provided NFT but fails to check if the NFT is already present in the `ManagedNFTs` array. This allows the owner to accidentally add the same NFT multiple times. When the owner later calls `releaseManagedNFT` to remove the NFT and transfer it out, the function iterates through `ManagedNFTs`, removes the first matching entry, and immediately `break`s out of the loop. This leaves any duplicate entries in the array. Because the NFT has been transferred away, the contract is no longer its owner. During the next withdrawal cycle, `withdrawFromManagedNFTs` will iterate over the stale duplicate entry and call `withdrawBalancesTo` on the released NFT. This call will revert because `LiquidInfrastructureNFT.withdrawBalancesTo` requires the caller to be the owner or approved. The revert permanently blocks the withdrawal cycle at that index. Furthermore, the owner cannot simply call `releaseManagedNFT` again to remove the stale entry, because `releaseManagedNFT` attempts to call `transferFrom` on the NFT, which will also revert since the contract no longer owns it. The only recovery is to somehow transfer the NFT back to the contract.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence clearly supports the hypothesis. `addManagedNFT` pushes to `ManagedNFTs` without deduplication. `releaseManagedNFT` uses a `break` statement after the first removal. `withdrawFromManagedNFTs` iterates over `ManagedNFTs` and calls an external contract that enforces ownership, which will revert for the stale entry.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function checks ownership but does not check if `nftContract` is already in the `ManagedNFTs` array, allowing duplicates to be pushed.

The `break` statement ensures only the first occurrence of the NFT is removed. If duplicates exist, they remain in the array. Additionally, the `transferFrom` call means the contract loses ownership, making subsequent calls to `releaseManagedNFT` for the same NFT revert.
Iterating over a stale entry will call `withdrawBalancesTo` on an NFT the contract no longer owns, causing a revert and halting the withdrawal process.
This function enforces that the caller is the owner or approved. Since the contract transferred the NFT during `releaseManagedNFT`, this check will fail and revert the transaction.

calling_sequence: Owner calls addManagedNFT(nft) -> nft added to ManagedNFTs
Owner calls addManagedNFT(nft) again -> duplicate nft added to ManagedNFTs
Owner calls releaseManagedNFT(nft, to) -> nft transferred, first entry removed, loop breaks
Anyone calls withdrawFromManagedNFTs(num) -> loop reaches stale duplicate entry
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path starts with `addManagedNFT` allowing duplicates. It proceeds to `releaseManagedNFT` where the `break` statement leaves the duplicate in the array while transferring ownership away. Finally, `withdrawFromManagedNFTs` accesses the stale entry and calls `withdrawBalancesTo`, which reverts because the contract is no longer the owner.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is highly realistic as it relies on a simple administrative mistake (calling `addManagedNFT` twice for the same address, perhaps due to a UI retry or script error). Once the mistake is made and the NFT is later released, the protocol is permanently bricked. The owner cannot fix it by calling `releaseManagedNFT` again because the `transferFrom` call inside it will revert.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High impact. A single duplicate entry, once released, permanently blocks all future withdrawals from all managed NFTs that come after it in the array. This freezes the revenue collection mechanism of the protocol, directly impacting all token holders.
recommended_mitigations: Add a check in `addManagedNFT` to ensure the NFT is not already in the `ManagedNFTs` array before pushing.
Remove the `break` statement in `releaseManagedNFT` so that all occurrences of the NFT are removed from the array.
Consider using a mapping or EnumerableSet to track managed NFTs instead of a raw array to prevent duplicates by design.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20 and a LiquidInfrastructureNFT. 2. Transfer the NFT to the ERC20 contract. 3. Call `addManagedNFT(nft)` twice from the owner account. 4. Call `releaseManagedNFT(nft, owner)` to release it. 5. Call `withdrawFromManagedNFTs(10)` and observe the transaction revert.
